title: SQL Interview · 10 drills
subtitle: HAVING · windows · JOIN traps · min-in-group · LAG · DQ

levels.title: Before practice · SQL execution order
levels.instruction: The server does not run clauses in the order you read them.
recallTips: FROM — where rows come from (tables / JOIN)
WHERE — filter raw rows (before GROUP BY)
GROUP BY — collapse into groups + aggregates
HAVING — filter on aggregates (after GROUP BY)
ORDER BY — sort at the end
levels.recallMnemonic: Mnemonic: FWGHSO — «From Where Group Having Select Order» (not how you read it!)

vizs.over: window: does NOT collapse rows — ROW_NUMBER / LAG

vizs.title: Traps
vizs.footnote: Mnemonic FWGHSO — not the order you type.

levels.title: HAVING vs WHERE

mission.title: HR analytics
mission.brief: Table core.employees: many people per department. First GROUP BY + AVG(salary), then drop low-paying departments.
mission.ask: Filter on an aggregate — which keyword after GROUP BY?

sampleTable.caption: Sales (1850) fails the 2000 threshold. WHERE won't work — we filter grouped rows.

columnGlossary.avg_salary: Average salary per department after GROUP BY

levels.controlTip: Mission and table above. Fill the SQL gap.
recallTips: WHERE — filters rows BEFORE grouping (per-person salary)
GROUP BY department + AVG(salary) — per department
HAVING — drops groups below threshold (not WHERE!)
levels.recallMnemonic: Rule: WHERE = rows · HAVING = groups after aggregate
levels.template:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM core.employees
GROUP BY department
____ AVG(salary) > 2000;

given.label: Given · full prompt
given.note: Use the table above as input; blanks complete the code/SQL below.
given.code:
# Context
Table core.employees: many people per department. First GROUP BY + AVG(salary), then drop low-paying departments.

department | avg_salary
--- | ---
IT | 3200
HR | 2100
Sales | 1850
# Sales (1850) fails the 2000 threshold. WHERE won't work — we filter grouped rows.
given.expect: Filter on an aggregate — which keyword after GROUP BY?

levels.title: ROW_NUMBER · top-1 per group
levels.instruction: Procurement: cheapest vendor quote per (category_code, tier).

mission.title: Marketplace · RFQ
mission.brief: Vendors submit quotes for the same category and quality tier. Need one row per (category_code, tier) with minimum unit_price. Standard live-coding pattern (min per group) — no fantasy tables.
mission.ask: Grain = one winner per (category_code, tier). What do we rank by?

sampleTable.caption: Same pattern as public «cheapest SKU / listing» tasks — B2B column names.

columnGlossary.in result?: Hint: row kept after min per group

levels.why: On interviews: 1) restate the prompt, 2) draw grain, 3) then OVER (PARTITION … ORDER …).
levels.template:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT quote_id, category_code, tier, unit_price,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      ____ BY category_code, tier
      ____ BY unit_price ____
    ) AS rn
  FROM staging.vendor_quotes
)
SELECT quote_id, category_code, tier, unit_price
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = ____;
hints: Group = PARTITION BY category_code, tier (rows stay).
Cheapest → ORDER BY unit_price ASC, then rn = 1.
levels.controlTip: Mission and table above. SQL skeleton below.

given.label: Given · full prompt
given.note: Use the table above as input; blanks complete the code/SQL below.
given.code:
# Context
Vendors submit quotes for the same category and quality tier. Need one row per (category_code, tier) with minimum unit_price. Standard live-coding pattern (min per group) — no fantasy tables.

quote_id | category_code | tier | unit_price | in result?
--- | --- | --- | --- | ---
Q-101 | ELEC | A | 12.50 | —
Q-102 | ELEC | A | 9.99 | ✓ min
Q-201 | ELEC | B | 15.00 | ✓ only
Q-301 | HOME | A | 8.00 | ✓ only
# Same pattern as public «cheapest SKU / listing» tasks — B2B column names.
given.expect: Grain = one winner per (category_code, tier). What do we rank by?

resultTrace.title: What remains after rn = 1
steps: ORDER BY unit_price ASC — cheapest = rn 1
WHERE rn = 1 — one row per group

levels.title: Min price per group (SKU)
levels.instruction: Same pattern as S2, but SKU catalog: is_active filter + result sort.

mission.title: Catalog / pricing
mission.brief: Among active rows: for each (power, age) pair — minimum price. Sort: power↓ age↓.
mission.ask: Raw rows in the table below. Find 3 final SKUs, then fill SQL.

sampleTable.caption: Expected result (3 rows): (10,20,40) → (8,40,25) → (5,45,10). Skip is_active=0 even if price is lowest.

columnGlossary.sku_id: Row ID in the price list (one seller offer)
columnGlossary.age: Batch / series age (product attribute, not person age)

levels.why: Point at winners in the table first — then PARTITION BY power, age and ORDER BY price ASC.
levels.template:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT sku_id, power, age, price,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER(
           PARTITION BY ____, ____
           ORDER BY price ____
         ) AS rn
  FROM sku_prices
  WHERE is_active = ____
)
SELECT sku_id, power, age, price
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY power ____, age DESC;
levels.controlTip: Mission and table above. SQL skeleton below.

given.label: Given · full prompt
given.note: Use the table above as input; blanks complete the code/SQL below.
given.code:
# Context
Among active rows: for each (power, age) pair — minimum price. Sort: power↓ age↓.

sku_id | power | age | price | is_active | in result?
--- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---
77 | 5 | 45 | 10 | 1 | ✓ min
12 | 5 | 45 | 50 | 1 | —
99 | 5 | 45 | 120 | 1 | —
43 | 8 | 40 | 25 | 1 | ✓ min
55 | 8 | 40 | 80 | 1 | —
3 | 8 | 40 | 5 | 0 | inactive
…
# Expected result (3 rows): (10,20,40) → (8,40,25) → (5,45,10). Skip is_active=0 even if price is lowest.
given.expect: Raw rows in the table below. Find 3 final SKUs, then fill SQL.

resultTrace.title: Min price per power×age
steps: ORDER BY price ASC → rn=1 = cheapest

levels.title: LAG · previous session
levels.instruction: For each session — previous session time for the same user_id.

mission.title: Product · retention
mission.brief: Table core.user_sessions: user_id, session_start. PM wants prev_session — when this user was online BEFORE the current row.
mission.ask: Which function looks at the previous row in the window?

sampleTable.caption: PARTITION BY user_id · ORDER BY session_start — timeline per user.

columnGlossary.prev_session: Expected LAG — previous session for this user

hints: NOW() is «now», not the past session.

given.label: Given · full prompt
given.note: Use the table above as input; blanks complete the code/SQL below.
given.code:
# Context
Table core.user_sessions: user_id, session_start. PM wants prev_session — when this user was online BEFORE the current row.

user_id | session_start | prev_session
--- | --- | ---
42 | 2026-01-01 10:00 | NULL
42 | 2026-01-03 14:00 | 2026-01-01 10:00
42 | 2026-01-05 09:00 | 2026-01-03 14:00
# PARTITION BY user_id · ORDER BY session_start — timeline per user.
given.expect: Which function looks at the previous row in the window?

levels.title: What's wrong?
levels.instruction: Classic live-coding mistake: MAX in WHERE.

mission.title: Interviewer prompt
mission.brief: «Show customer_id where max order amount > 500, sort by amount». Candidate wrote the code below — find the main bug.

levels.buggyCode:
SELECT customer_id, MAX(amount) AS maximum_order_amount
FROM orders
WHERE MAX(amount) > 500
GROUP BY maximum_order_amount
ORDER BY 2 DESC;
levels.prompt: Pick the main bug (there are several, but one key diagnosis).
options: Aggregates in WHERE + GROUP BY on alias — need HAVING and GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY 2 DESC is forbidden in all databases
MAX always requires a window function
levels.explainOk: WHERE MAX(...) is illegal; alias in GROUP BY too. Correct: GROUP BY customer_id HAVING MAX(amount) > 500.

levels.title: JOIN · row duplication

mission.title: Setup
mission.brief: users: 10 unique user_id. orders: many rows per user. LEFT JOIN users → orders — how many rows in the result?

levels.joinCaption: u1 has 2 orders → duplicated after LEFT JOIN. u2 has no orders → one row with NULLs. Total 4 rows from 3 users.
levels.question: Can a LEFT JOIN result have MORE than 10 rows?
options: Yes — if one user has several orders, the user row is duplicated
No — LEFT JOIN never increases row count of the left table
levels.explanation: 1:N relationship inflates cardinality. A common cause of duplicate metrics.

levels.title: COALESCE · NULL in CORE
levels.instruction: On staging layer: replace NULL in amount with 0 before loading to core.

mission.title: DQ · staging → core
mission.brief: In staging.orders some amount = NULL (source glitch). In marts, sums cannot be NULL — default 0.

sampleTable.caption: Typical DQ requirement on DE/AE interviews.

levels.controlTip: Mission above — fill function and default.
hints: Alternative: CASE WHEN amount IS NULL THEN 0.

given.label: Given · full prompt
given.note: Use the table above as input; blanks complete the code/SQL below.
given.code:
# Context
In staging.orders some amount = NULL (source glitch). In marts, sums cannot be NULL — default 0.

order_id | amount (raw) | cleaned_amount
--- | --- | ---
O-1 | 120.50 | 120.50
O-2 | NULL | 0
O-3 | 80 | 80
# Typical DQ requirement on DE/AE interviews.

levels.title: Deduplication
levels.instruction: Keep one row per user_id — freshest by updated_at.

mission.title: Fintech · CDC duplicates
mission.brief: staging.users gets repeat loads for the same user_id. Core needs one current row per key.
mission.ask: PARTITION BY what? ORDER BY what DESC? rn = ?

sampleTable.caption: ROW_NUMBER … WHERE rn = 1 — standard dedup on interviews.

columnGlossary.user_id: Business key — one current row per user

levels.template:
WITH RankedData AS (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY ____
           ORDER BY ____ DESC
         ) AS rn
  FROM staging.users
)
SELECT *
FROM RankedData
WHERE rn = ____;
hints: Newest = ORDER BY updated_at DESC, rn=1.

given.label: Given · full prompt
given.note: Use the table above as input; blanks complete the code/SQL below.
given.code:
# Context
staging.users gets repeat loads for the same user_id. Core needs one current row per key.

user_id | updated_at | keep?
--- | --- | ---
U-9 | 2026-06-01 | —
U-9 | 2026-06-10 | ✓ newest
U-9 | 2026-06-05 | —
# ROW_NUMBER … WHERE rn = 1 — standard dedup on interviews.
given.expect: PARTITION BY what? ORDER BY what DESC? rn = ?

resultTrace.title: One row per user_id
steps: ORDER BY updated_at DESC → rn=1 = newest

levels.title: MATCH · windows vs aggregates
levels.instruction: Match each tool to its behavior — look at the tables first, not words alone.
levels.studyIntro: Step 1 · see the result. Two schemas below: GROUP BY collapses rows, OVER does not. Then ranking. Read the tables → then match pairs.

studyVizs.title: Same orders — two different results
studyVizs.footnote: In interviews, draw exactly this: left = rows gone, right = new column, rows stay.

studyVizs.title: Same scores — three different behaviors

notes.note: Ana+Bob tie at 1 → both 1; Cal+Dan tie at 3 (2 skipped)

studyVizs.footnote: ORDER BY score DESC. Tie = same score → RANK/DENSE match, ROW_NUMBER does not.

levels.title: Query execution order
levels.instruction: Arrange as the server sees it (not as you write it).
levels.recallIntro: Map first. Same order as level S0 · SQL map at the start of this block.

studyViz.over: OVER() — after FROM; does not collapse rows

recallTips: ORDER BY — sort at the end
levels.recallMnemonic: FWGHSO: From → Where → Group → Having → Select → Order
levels.recallMapRef: 📍 Forgot? Open S0 «Before practice · SQL execution order» in this block's level list.
